findings.description: The NC Energy, Mineral & Land Resources logo/graphic is not rendered in the HTML version
findings.evidence: PDF page 1 shows the NC state logo with 'Energy, Mineral & Land Resources / ENVIRONMENTAL QUALITY' branding in the top left. The HTML version appears to omit this logo graphic.

findings.description: Page 2 in PDF contains only the bottom of page 1 content (footer area) and is mostly blank, but the HTML rendering shows the full header block repeated plus the beginning of the memorandum content again, creating duplicate content
findings.evidence: PDF page 2 is essentially blank with just a footer. HTML page 2 shows the full government header (PAT McCRORY, DONALD R. VAN DER VAART, TRACY DAVIS), MEMORANDUM heading, and repeats the memo fields (To, From, Subject) and introductory paragraphs that were already on page 1.

findings.description: The outline list on page 1 uses Roman numerals with periods in the PDF but the HTML rendering matches this format adequately
findings.evidence: Both versions show I. History/Background, II. March 10, 2016..., III. Written Public Comments Summary, IV. Attachments

findings.description: Page 4 in PDF is mostly blank (bottom of page 3 content area) but the HTML version shows it as a continuation page with full content from the History/Background section and into the Meeting and Oral Comments Summary
findings.evidence: PDF page 4 is largely empty with just footer. HTML page 4 contains substantial content including bullet points about CAMA process and the beginning of Section II.

findings.description: Page 6 in the HTML collapses content from PDF pages 5-6, showing oral comment topics and written comment summary topics together
findings.evidence: The HTML rendering combines content across page boundaries differently than the PDF, though the content itself is preserved

findings.description: In the Attachments section, the PDF uses Roman numerals aligned with periods (I., II., III., etc.) while the HTML version also uses Roman numerals but with slightly different list formatting
findings.evidence: PDF shows attachment list as formatted Roman numeral list with tab stops. HTML shows similar content but as a simpler list.

findings.description: The MEMORANDUM title is styled as h1, which is acceptable, but the document outline section 'I. History/Background' uses the same heading text as the PDF. However, the outline list on page 1 uses Roman numerals I-IV which in the HTML uses <ol type='I'> but the Attachments section on page 4 also uses <ol type='I'> starting from I, which matches the PDF structure. No actual hierarchy breakage.
findings.evidence: Heading structure is reasonable: h1 for MEMORANDUM, h2 for sections I-IV. This is acceptable.

findings.description: The first image has alt text 'NC Department of Environmental Quality Logo' which is a reasonable description of the NC logo. However, pages 2-4 have images with empty alt text (alt=''), which appear to be repeated full-page images that are likely artifacts of the conversion process rather than meaningful content images.
findings.evidence: Pages 2-4:

findings.description: The HTML has a broken list structure where the bulleted list from the Oral Comments Summary (Section II) spans across pages 2 and 3, but the page footer div is inserted INSIDE the <ul> element between list items, and a second <figure> image tag is also inserted mid-list. Similarly, the Written Public Comments list (Section III) has a page footer and figure inserted mid-list between pages 3 and 4. While the content itself is not duplicated, this creates malformed HTML that breaks the semantic list structure.
findings.evidence: The <ul> starting in Section II has a <div class='page-footer'> and <figure> tag inserted between <li> elements, splitting the list across page boundaries improperly.

findings.description: The governor's name in the HTML header is rendered as 'PAT McCrORY' with inconsistent capitalization, whereas the PDF shows 'PAT McCRORY'.
There are two nested <main> elements in the HTML, which is invalid HTML structure but does not affect content fidelity.
